questions.question: Explain why Randomized Quicksort has expected O(n log n) time complexity while standard Quicksort can degrade to O(n²).
keyPoints: Standard Quicksort uses fixed pivot (first/last element)
Sorted input creates unbalanced partitions (n-1, 0)
Randomized Quicksort randomly selects pivot
Any element has 1/n probability of being pivot
This ensures balanced partitions on average
Probability analysis using indicator variables yields O(n log n)

questions.question: Describe the partition procedure used in Randomized Quicksort with an example.
keyPoints: Choose random pivot element
Initialize two pointers or use Lomuto scheme
Iterate through array comparing elements with pivot
Move elements smaller than pivot to left partition
Move elements greater than pivot to right partition
Place pivot in its correct position

questions.question: What is the probability that two specific elements are compared in Randomized Quicksort? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Two elements are compared when one becomes pivot before the other is separated
Elements are compared at most once in the entire algorithm
For any pair, probability of comparison is 1/2
This is because pivot is equally likely to be any element between them
Total expected comparisons = C(n,2) × 1/2 = O(n log n)

questions.question: Differentiate between average case, best case, and worst case of Randomized Quicksort.
keyPoints: Best case: O(n log n) when pivot always divides array into two equal halves
Average case: O(n log n) - expected with random pivots
Worst case: O(n²) - occurs when pivot is always smallest/largest element
Probability of worst case is 1/n! (extremely rare)
Standard Quicksort has predictable worst-case on sorted input
Randomized Quicksort makes worst-case input unlikely

questions.question: Explain why Randomized Quicksort is not considered a stable sorting algorithm.
keyPoints: Stability means equal elements maintain their relative order
During partition, elements around pivot are swapped
Elements with same key value may be reordered
Pivot exchange can change positions of equal elements
Standard implementation does not preserve original order
For stability, additional tracking information is needed

questions.question: What is tail recursion optimization in Randomized Quicksort and why is it useful?
keyPoints: Optimizes the second recursive call to reduce stack space
Converts one recursive call into iteration
Sort the smaller partition first recursively
Then iterate on the larger partition
Reduces worst-case stack from O(n) to O(log n)
Useful when memory is limited

questions.question: Compare Randomized Quicksort with Merge Sort in terms of time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Both have O(n log n) average time complexity
Merge Sort is stable; Randomized Quicksort is not
Merge Sort requires O(n) extra space; Randomized Quicksort uses O(log n)
Randomized Quicksort is in-place; Merge Sort is not
Merge Sort guarantees O(n log n); Randomized Quicksort has O(n²) worst-case
Merge Sort uses divide-conquer-combine; Randomized Quicksort divides and combines during partition

questions.question: Derive the expected time complexity of Randomized Quicksort using probability analysis. Show how indicator random variables are used to calculate expected number of comparisons.
keyPoints: Define indicator variable Xij = 1 if elements ai and aj are compared
Probability of comparison: P(Xij = 1) = 2/(j-i+1) = 1/2 for distinct pivots
Simplify: For any pair, probability is 1/2 (pivot chosen between them)
Expected comparisons = sum of E[Xij] over all pairs
Number of pairs = n(n-1)/2
E[comparisons] = n(n-1)/2 × 1/2 ≈ n²/4 for naive analysis
Correct analysis gives 2n ln n ≈ 1.39n log n
By linearity of expectation: T(n) = 2(n-1) + T(k) + T(n-k-1)
Solving recurrence yields O(n log n) expected time

questions.question: Write the complete Randomized Quicksort algorithm and explain each step. Also analyze its time and space complexity with proof.
keyPoints: Algorithm: RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)
If p < r: q = RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, q-1)
RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, q+1, r)
RANDOMIZED-PARTITION swaps pivot with last element, then partitions
Time complexity: Best/Average O(n log n), Worst O(n²)
Space: O(log n) average for recursion stack
Proof of O(n log n) uses recurrence T(n) = T(k) + T(n-k-1) + O(n)
With random k (uniform distribution), expected value solves to O(n log n)

questions.question: What is three-way partitioning in Quicksort? Explain how it handles duplicate elements efficiently and write the algorithm.
keyPoints: Three-way partitioning divides array into three parts: < pivot, = pivot, > pivot
Also called Dutch National Flag algorithm
Handles arrays with many duplicate values efficiently
When all elements are equal, runs in O(n) instead of O(n²)
Algorithm uses three pointers: lt (less than), gt (greater than), i (current)
Initialize: lt = low, gt = high-1, i = low
While i <= gt: compare arr[i] with pivot and adjust pointers
Time complexity: O(n log n) for distinct elements, O(n) for all equal
Space complexity remains O(log n)

questions.question: Explain Introsort. How does it guarantee O(n log n) worst-case time complexity? Compare it with Randomized Quicksort.
keyPoints: Introsort = INTROspective SORT: hybrid of Quicksort + Heapsort + Insertion Sort
Starts with regular Quicksort (not randomized)
Tracks recursion depth; if exceeds threshold (2 × log n), switches to Heapsort
Heapsort guarantees O(n log n) worst-case
Also uses insertion sort for small subarrays (< 16 elements)
Comparison with Randomized Quicksort:
Both have same average O(n log n)
Introsort guarantees O(n log n) worst-case
Randomized Quicksort has O(n²) worst-case but with negligible probability
Introsort uses more complex logic; Randomized Quicksort is simpler
Randomization provides probabilistic guarantee; Introsort provides deterministic guarantee
